anti_pattern_rules: Keyword extraction is forbidden in any form: extracting words from text A, searching for them in text B, and drawing any conclusion from matches. See PM-0001, PM-0002.
Scripts present information for human review. Scripts never assign verdicts based on mechanical text matching.
No automated status assignment (CLEAR, FLAGGED, NEEDS_REVIEW, KEEP, SKIP) based on text matching.

COVERED verdict anti-patterns — three forbidden shortcuts: (1) Topic-level match: 'book argues X, finding argues X, therefore covered' is WRONG. Must name the specific evidence the finding brings AND where the book already presents that same specific evidence. (2) 'Same argument' without evidence comparison: 'ch1 makes this exact argument' is not enough. Does the book already present this specific scholar's framing, this specific quote, this specific example? If any of these are new, verdict is new_evidence_existing_argument, not covered. (3) Implicit coverage: 'the concept is implicit in the framework' or 'this is a variant of what is covered' means the book does NOT state it — therefore NOT covered. Any of these prevent a covered verdict: a named concept the book doesn't use, a specific mechanism the book doesn't describe, a new scope claim, or a new specific source text (e.g., book has Mark-Homer mimesis but finding has Mark-Aeneid — different source, not a variant). Test: does the inventory list this exact item? If you must argue it is 'implied by' or 'a variant of' an existing item, it is NOT covered.

The verdict matrix has two axes — is the ARGUMENT new, and is the EVIDENCE new? covered: argument in book AND evidence in book (or Q&A addresses it). new_evidence_existing_argument: argument in book, evidence NOT in book — new evidence to bolster an existing argument. new_argument_existing_evidence: argument NOT in book, evidence IS in book — someone looked at the same source we cite and drew a different conclusion. Often the most valuable: data already verified, only the argument is new. Do NOT downgrade to new_evidence_existing_argument or covered just because the evidence appears in the book. new_evidence_and_argument: argument NOT in book, evidence NOT in book — both need evaluation.

findings.description: The speaker notes that a first-century Galilean Jewish person would have understood "salvation" through the anticipated Messiah as "political temporal salvation"—the expectation that the savior would overcome oppressors in battle. This directly supports the book's argument that the "Kingdom of God" must be understood as a political institution requiring historical and political context.
findings.quote: a first century uh Galilean Jewish person hearing this would have understood this to refer to political temporal salvation that this savior was going to overcome their oppressors in battle

findings.video_title: Come say hi!
findings.description: McClellan affirms that deification of rulers was standard practice in the ancient world, noting Psalm 45's explicit address of the king as "God" — a practice that "would have been kind of part for the course in that time period." This contextualizes "Son of God" as fitting within broader ancient royal theological frameworks where titles of divinity were extended to monarchs.
findings.quote: there's definitely a deification going on to some degree we've got Psalm 45 where it says I address my word words to The King and then says oh God uh and and says oh God your God uh so there's a explicit vocative address of the king as God um and that and that would have been kind of part for the course in that time period

findings.video_title: Livestream with Kipp & Vish
findings.description: The scholars establish that no Jewish text or tradition prior to 30 CE expected a suffering, dying, and resurrecting Messiah. This absence in the Jewish theological framework underscores the need for an alternative interpretive lens—potentially the Greco-Christian framework the book proposes—to account for how resurrection theology was conceptualized.
findings.quote: There's no evidence any Jewish person prior to the 30s CE believed the Messiah would suffer and die and come back to life.

findings.video_title: March Livestream
findings.description: McClellan notes that the Gospel of John contains Platonic ideas, reinforcing the Greco-Christian philosophical framework within which the Johannine theology of Jesus operates.
findings.quote: in John you have some platonic ideas

findings.video_title: Livestream with Jacob Berman
findings.description: McClellan discusses how Daniel 7:14 was reinterpreted in Greek translation to identify the Son of Man with the Ancient of Days (through a textual variant), showing that Hellenistic Jewish interpreters already applied categories of divine succession and identity to the Son of Man figure—supporting how Jesus could be understood through Greco-Christian royal-divine frameworks.
findings.quote: in Daniel 7 we have this reference to one like a son of man who comes in the Clouds Of Heaven... there's an old Greek translation that changes hos to just host one letter difference so now it says the one like a son of man comes in clouds... as the Ancient of Days... which would seem to be conflating their identities

findings.video_title: Livestream with Jacob Berman
findings.description: McClellan explains that Divine Images—a concept familiar from both Greco-Roman and Jewish contexts—manifested divine power and authority without being identical with the deity itself. This provides the conceptual framework for understanding how first-century audiences would interpret Jesus as "Son of God," exercising divine functions without ontological identity with God, consistent with Hellenistic royal theology.
findings.quote: Divine Images which all of them would have been familiar with would have grown up with and it would have been very intuitive to them that Divine Images are not the deity but manifest the power The Authority the presence of the deity and can be referred to as if they were the deity

findings.video_title: Livestream with Jacob Berman
findings.description: McClellan discusses how in first-century Jewish thought, authorized bearers of the divine name (like Metatron in rabbinic tradition) could exercise divine authority and be referred to as divine without being identical with God, providing a parallel framework for understanding Jesus's divine agency within a Hellenistic model of divine mediation.
findings.quote: the authorized possessor or bearer of the Divine name exercises God's power and authority and can identify with God... they can refer to themselves as God not because they are God but because they... have been enlivened and activated by the possession of the Divine name

findings.video_title: A common mistranslation in John 10:33
findings.description: Scholar Dan McClellan's analysis of John 10:33 clarifies that Jesus is accused of making himself "divine" or "a god," not claiming to be "the God" of Israel. Jesus's response citing Psalm 82 (others called "gods" by Scripture) makes sense only under this reading. McClellan concludes: "at no point whatsoever in this chapter does Jesus claim to be God." This scholarly analysis supports the book's interpretation of "Son of God" as a royal/elevated divine title distinct from claiming absolute godhood, consistent with Hellenistic royal ideology.
findings.quote: if those to whom the word of God came can be called Gods then how can it be blasphemy for me to say I'm the Son of God... at no point whatsoever in this chapter does Jesus claim to be God

findings.video_title: How can Jesus call God “my God”?
findings.description: The speaker argues that Jesus's divine titles (like being called "God" in Hebrews 1:8-9) should be understood in Hellenistic royal framework. Citing Psalm 45, he notes that the human king is called "Elohim" (divine) because "this human king has been ordained by God and basically functions as God's representative on earth," and argues "The same thing is being done in Hebrews where Jesus is being recognized as divine in the same sense that the human king was recognized as divine." This directly supports the book's thesis that Jesus's titles and theology should be understood through Greco-Christian institutional frameworks.
findings.quote: this human king has been ordained by God and basically functions as God's representative on earth. They are represented as divine as an Elohim. And this is something that is very common in this time and in this place...The same thing is being done in Hebrews where Jesus is being recognized as divine in the same sense that the human king was recognized as divine.

findings.video_title: Is Jesus claiming to be God in John 8:58?
findings.description: The speaker cites Michael Peppard's "The Son of God in the Roman World" to establish that in the Greco-Roman world, the relationship between deity and humanity was understood as a spectrum rather than a binary—directly supporting the book's framework for understanding divine titles like "Son of God" in Hellenistic context.
findings.quote: And a wonderful book on this topic is Michael Peppard's book, The Son of God in the Roman World, that talks precisely about the fact that in this time and place, the relationship between deity and humanity was more of a spectrum than a dichotomy.

findings.video_title: Where does Jesus ever claim to be God?
findings.description: McClellan argues that Jesus's appropriation of divine titles ("Alpha and Omega," "I am," etc.) derives from his possession of the Divine name, understood through a Greco-Roman framework of divine intermediaries and agents who could bear God's authority and speak on God's behalf without being God himself. This framework grounds Jesus's titles in Mediterranean religious ontology rather than later Trinitarian philosophy, supporting the book's Greco-Christian interpretive lens.
findings.quote: Jesus is authorized to appropriate these titles not because Jesus is God but because as the authorized possessor of the Divine name Jesus speaks on behalf of God Bears God's power agency and authority and manifests or reifies God's presence

findings.video_title: Where does Jesus ever claim to be God?
findings.description: The concept of "Divine Images" in Greco-Roman religion is presented as the key interpretive framework: Jesus functions as a divine image bearing God's name, allowing him to manifest God's presence—a model already embedded in ancient Mediterranean ontology. This directly supports understanding Jesus through Hellenistic rather than purely Jewish conceptual categories.
findings.quote: Jesus is saying here is that I am the authorized bearer of the Divine name I bear God's Authority I speak on behalf of God I can even furtively identify with God and as a walking talking sensient Divine image to see me is to see God... this is what makes this so intuitive it's because the entire world was steeped in this ontology

findings.video_title: Where does Jesus ever claim to be God?
findings.description: McClellan identifies a tradition in "Greco-Roman period Judaism" of high-ranking angels exercising God's power and authority through possession of God's name—providing explicit evidence for the Greco-Roman Jewish theological context in which early Christology developed, reinforcing the book's thesis that Greco-Christian rather than purely Judeo-Christian models are appropriate.
findings.quote: this concept was related to a high-ranking angel that exercised God's Own power and authority because they bore or possessed God's name

findings.video_title: Where does Jesus ever claim to be God?
findings.description: McClellan explicitly rejects imposing later theological frameworks (Trinitarian substance/essence philosophy) onto first-century texts, arguing instead for understanding Jesus within contemporary Greco-Roman Jewish concepts of intermediaries and divine agents—methodologically aligned with the book's critique of dominant scholarly models.
findings.quote: there are readings that do not require we just arbitrarily retroject much later developed philosophical Frameworks into an earlier period where those Frameworks did not yet exist

findings.video_title: Come say hi!
findings.description: McClellan describes Trinity development as fundamentally dependent on Greek philosophical concepts (homoousios/"same substance," distinguishing person from being). He emphasizes that "the Trinity arises as a need um to try to intellectualize and make the gospel philosophically palatable uh because they're trying to appeal to the Roman intelligencia," and that Christianity became "wedded to Greek philosophy" in its christological debates, a pattern that supports the book's argument about Greco-Christian theological framework.
findings.quote: Trinity was something that developed over time as people came up with ways to make sense of what they were trying to unify in the New Testament... you had some ideas that uh like like homo Usos uh same substance that's that's the the term that we have at NAA uh in the 2 and third Century

findings.video_title: Come say hi!
findings.description: McClellan notes that concepts of deity immateriality and incorporeality derive from Greek philosophical influence, not Jewish tradition. "Christianity adopts uh a love of Greek Philosophy from GRE Roman Jewish helenistic Judaism... Christianity says um basically is wedded to Greek philosophy" while Rabbinic Judaism "goes another Direction returns to the Hebrew Bible."
findings.quote: Christianity adopts uh a love of Greek Philosophy from GRE Roman Jewish helenistic Judaism and rinic Judaism goes another Direction returns to the Hebrew Bible

findings.video_title: Maklelan Livestream
findings.description: McClellan presents his scholarly "hot take" that Jesus's paradoxical divine status (being simultaneously God and not God) was "perfectly natural in the first century CE Greco Roman Jewish world" due to the conceptual framework of Divine Images. He calls this "image christology" and emphasizes that the logic of Divine Images "just made sense to everybody because everybody had Divine Images." This directly supports the book's thesis that understanding Jesus requires a Greco-Christian framework, providing a specific mechanism by which Hellenistic-Roman concepts enabled comprehension of Christ's divine nature.
findings.quote: Jesus was just conceptualized as a Divine image and that's called image christology... it relied on the logic of Divine Images and that just made sense to everybody because everybody had Divine Images

findings.video_title: Maklelan Livestream!
findings.description: High Christology developed through incorporation of middle Platonic logos theology into understanding Jesus as Messiah and Son of God, showing how Greek philosophical frameworks shaped early Christian titles and theology.
findings.quote: it developed slowly over time as people were um divonizing Jesus more and more and pushing the origins of his relationship with God further back past his birth back to the creation of the world. incorporating kind of a um middle platonic logos theology into uh into the the notion of Jesus as the Messiah and the son of God

findings.video_title: Maklelan Livestream!
findings.description: The Angel of the Lord functioned as a conceptual template for early Christian architects in framing Jesus's divine mediation, showing how existing Jewish theological concepts were repurposed to express Jesus's divine status within Greco-Christian frameworks.
findings.quote: I think that the concept of the angel of the Lord was a conceptual template that the uh architects of the uh earliest conceptualizations of Jesus used to frame what they were doing.

findings.video_title: November Livestream
findings.description: The speaker discusses divine images logic as an intuitive framework explaining how Jesus could be both identified with God and distinguished from God. This parallels arguments about how Hellenistic categories (rather than purely Jewish ones) account for early Christology.
findings.quote: the intuitive logic of Divine Images is probably undergirding a lot of the different ideas that people had about how Jesus could both be identified with God and distinguished from God... just like the logic of Divine Images when you are in the presence of a Divine image that's not the deity but at the same time it is the deity and to see a Divine image is to see God

findings.video_title: November Livestream
findings.description: The speaker argues that the Greek translation of Daniel 7 supports divine identification: "the one like a son of man does not come to the Ancient of Days in the Greek translation uh the one like a son of man comes as the Ancient of Days" — evidence that Greek translation traditions understood "one like a son of man" as identified with God-like status.
findings.quote: particularly the Greek translation of Daniel 7 uh where the son of man the one like a son of man does not come to the Ancient of Days in the Greek translation uh the one like a son of man comes as the Ancient of Days and whether that was intentional or not it seems to suggest uh some kind of identification of the two

findings.video_title: November Livestream
findings.description: The speaker discusses logos theology in John as rooted in Platonic emanationism: "the word the logos... is an emanation from God" and describes it using Philo's "second God" and Justin Martyr's "another God" — grounding the title "Son of God" in Greek philosophical rather than purely Jewish apocalyptic categories.
findings.quote: the word the logos uh you can go to Pho for a more thorough discussion of of this logos this second God as as Pho calls him another God as Justus martyr calls him uh is an emanation from God

findings.video_title: The significance of the crown of thorns #maklelan2645
findings.description: Early Christian artistic depictions (4th-century sarcophagi) portrayed Jesus wearing a royal leafy wreath crown associated with Hellenistic royalty and military victory, demonstrating that Christians understood Jesus through Hellenistic royal categories and reinterpreted his kingship as spiritual victory over death rather than political-military triumph.
findings.quote: the first time it is depicted in Christian iconography and art is in the middle of the 4th Century CE on a sarcophagus where it is not represented as thorny and spiky but as a leafy wreath crown...associated with royalty or with military Victory...Christians might have then appropriated that imagery to suggest that the real victory was Jesus's victory over death

findings.video_title: No, we’re not living in the last seconds of the last days
findings.description: The speaker argues that apocalyptic imagery in the Beast narrative should be understood as first-century CE political commentary on Roman emperors, not as future prophecy. This supports the book's framework of understanding early Christian theology and language in its Greco-Roman political context.
findings.quote: that is just late first century CE apocalyptic imagery that is encoding criticisms of Roman emperors from the first century CE it is not a prophecy about anything in the future

findings.video_title: Responding to questions about deification
findings.description: The speaker (maklelan) identifies theosis/divinization as a Greco-Roman theological innovation adopted by early Christianity, not a Hebrew tradition. This directly supports the book's argument that "Son of God" and divine status claims are rooted in Hellenistic rather than Jewish frameworks.
findings.quote: this is an idea that is primarily taking shape within Greco Roman period Judaism and then is picked up within Christianity and interpreted in a few different ways

findings.video_title: Responding to questions about deification
findings.description: The speaker traces the New Testament concept of believers becoming "sons of God" and achieving divine status to Greco-Roman postmortem deification practices, explicitly contrasting this with the Hebrew Bible's stance against human deification.
findings.quote: it is the result of the preservation of this older idea about ancestor worship and the deceased ancestors being deities combining with the Greco Roman period innovation of the idea of postmortem Divine reward

findings.video_title: Responding to questions about deification
findings.description: The Gospel of John's theology of Jesus's followers "becoming sons of God" and "becoming one with Jesus the same way that Jesus is one with God" is identified as part of this broader Greco-Roman deification tradition, providing theological grounding for the "Son of God" title's Hellenistic context.
findings.quote: within the Gospel of John with concepts of uh Jesus's followers becoming sons of God with concepts of Jesus's followers becoming one with Jesus the same way that Jesus is one with God

findings.video_title: Does Proverbs 30:4 mention the Son of God?
findings.description: The speaker establishes that "Son of God" terminology in Jewish tradition was applied to the Davidic king (citing Psalm 2:7), reinforcing the book's argument that "Son of God" is fundamentally a royal/political title rather than a purely theological one. The reference to Collins' *King and Messiah as Son of God* suggests scholarly support for analyzing the development of this terminology.
findings.quote: the other has to do with Psalms 2:7 you are my son this day I have begotten you that is later reread as a reference to Jesus as the Messiah but when it was originally written it was a reference to the davidic king who was considered the anointed

findings.video_title: Myths about Easter & Nicea in Today’s Episode of the @dataoverdogma Podcast
findings.description: The speaker acknowledges serious scholarly discussion regarding Greco-Roman influence on the resurrection narrative and broader Jesus traditions (pre-existence, birth, mortality), supporting the book's Greco-Christian interpretive framework.
findings.quote: there is a discussion to be had regarding the way the resurrection of Jesus the Traditions associated with the resurrection of Jesus and the broader story of Jesus's uh pre-existence and uh birth and mortality and everything there's a discussion to be had there regarding how there is influence from the Broadway Greco-Roman world and and other Traditions that may have been in circulation in that Greco-Roman world

findings.video_title: Maklelan Livestream
findings.description: McLelan describes John's Christology as adopting "a more philosophical approach that is adopting a middle platonic idea of the logos," positioning Jesus as "prior to his Incarnation was the logos that was God's rationality." This evidences John's engagement with Greek philosophical frameworks central to a Greco-Christian reading.
findings.quote: John is a more philosophical approach that is adopting a middle platonic idea of the logos so Jesus prior to his Incarnation was the logos that was God's rationality

findings.video_title: Maklelan Livestream
findings.description: McLelan confirms scholarly consensus that Kingdom of Heaven should be understood as earthly and political: "the idea of the Kingdom of Heaven is something that would be on earth and is something that they were trying to achieve for this temporal existence," supporting the book's interpretation of kingdom as a political institution rather than purely spiritual/heavenly.
findings.quote: the idea of the Kingdom of Heaven is something that would be on earth and is something that they were trying to achieve for this temporal existence

findings.video_title: Round-up of my account’s first 47 videos
findings.description: The speaker traces the development of divine titles for Jesus within Greco-Roman Jewish traditions. He describes how Daniel 7's "son of man" figure became a mediatory divine title in Greco-Roman Judaism before being applied to Christ, supporting the book's Greco-Christian framework for understanding Jesus's titles. The evidence about mediatory figures who possess and exercise God's authority within Greco-Roman pseudepigraphic literature directly strengthens the argument that Hellenistic theological frameworks, not merely Jewish apocalyptic tradition, shaped early Christian christology.
findings.quote: it seems that a tradition developed within Greco Roman period Judaism of a Messianic Divine mediator figure who was known as both Ancient of Days and the son of man and that tradition is taken up in the New Testament as a way to identify Christ as this mediatory figure

findings.video_title: Was Gehenna a Place for the Punishment of the Living?
findings.description: The speaker traces the development of Gehenna as a spiritual punishment concept to the Greco-Roman period, when the physical location became associated with postmortem divine punishment through figurative extension. This supports the book's argument that Hellenistic frameworks fundamentally shaped early Christian theological concepts.
findings.quote: these ideas developed in the greco-roman period when that physical location the valley of hinnom became associated by figurative extension with postmortem divine punishment

findings.video_title: Biblical contradictions & how to make them go away
findings.description: Maklelan demonstrates that Platonic philosophical frameworks fundamentally shaped early Christian theology, particularly regarding deity conceptualization. This supports the book's Greco-Christian interpretive framework by showing how Greek philosophical traditions were integrated into Christian thought from the earliest periods, establishing the intellectual foundations for understanding Jesus's titles and theological identity within a Greek philosophical context.
findings.quote: Christianity is built on a Foundation of Greek philosophy and particularly platonic streams of tradition within Greek philosophy particularly when we're talking about conceptualizations of deity... within Christianity it happened much earlier by Augustine we've already abandoned the notion of a corporeal visible deity

findings.video_title: Aron Ra: “Jesus Never Said He Was God”
findings.description: The speaker argues that language about authority-bearing and unity with God comes from "intermediary figures that are already well known within Greco-Roman period Jewish circles," supporting the book's Greco-Christian framework by demonstrating that such concepts had established precedent in the Hellenistic context rather than being unique claims to deity.
findings.quote: these things are being borrowed from intermediary figures that are already well known within Greco-Roman period Jewish circles and they were never understood to be identifying these intermediary figures as God themselves

findings.video_title: Does the Hebrew Bible Teach That God Has a Son?
findings.description: The speaker argues that Proverbs 30:4's reference to God's son requires "presupposing a tradition about a Divine Son of God" and represents "a Christian renegotiation of a pre-christian text." This supports the book's thesis that "Son of God" is fundamentally a Greco-Christian theological framework, not native to Hebrew tradition, and was retrofitted into Old Testament texts.
findings.quote: one must presuppose a tradition about a Divine Son of God who is also in some way identified with God themselves in order to read these texts the way this Creator reads them and this is a Christian renegotiation of a pre-christian text

findings.video_title: The Word is divine, not God
findings.description: The transcript demonstrates how early Christian thinkers explicitly engaged with Greek theological categories to understand Jesus's divinity, distinguishing between the supreme God (ho theos with article) and divine beings who participate in divinity (theos without article). This directly supports understanding Jesus's titles in Hellenistic rather than strictly Jewish monotheistic terms.
findings.quote: we have Jewish and Christian authors who not only did read something into it, they explicitly explain precisely why when the word theos is applied to the logos, the word you should omit the article... only the God holos with the article is the very God of Israel. But divinity can be extended to others and others can participate in divinity without being the very God of Israel.

findings.video_title: Responding to concerns with my video on omnipotence, omniscience & omnipresence
findings.description: Scholar documents that the Greek title "Pantocrator" was borrowed into Judaism through the Greek translation of the Hebrew Bible—not translating any Hebrew word but added as a newly adopted title for God. This supports the book's argument about Greco-Christian framework for understanding early Christian titles.
findings.quote: the title pantor this is a traditional uh title for Divinity that comes from Greek religion it was borrowed into Judaism through the ancient Greek translation of the Hebrew Bible which actually just adds it in it's not translating any Hebrew word it's just being added in and adopted as a title for God

findings.video_title: Responding to concerns with my video on omnipotence, omniscience & omnipresence
findings.description: Scholar distinguishes between titles and the later philosophical doctrines built upon them: "the doctrine of omnipotence is a carefully worked out philosophical and conceptual package it's not just a title but an entire package." This supports the book's approach to Greek titles like "Son of God" as carrying institutional meaning without necessarily requiring later theological development.
findings.quote: we see this package being developed within early Christianity as early apologists and patristic authors and philosophers build from this scratch Foundation of this title

findings.video_title: Come say hi!
findings.description: Dan describes Jewish concepts of exalted intermediary figures (Metatron, vice-regent) who sit next to God's throne, bear God's name, and manifest God's divine authority. These Jewish precedents for exalted agents exercising divine power provide conceptual background for understanding how "Son of God" functions as a title for an exalted figure in early Christianity.
findings.quote: there are certain kinds of intermediary who represented as holding a special place uh next to God who somehow exercises God's power and authority and even can manifest God's presence... Metatron who is who Bears adonai's name has a name similar to adoni and so that's actually referring to a different uh figure named Adonai

findings.video_title: Divine Images, the Angel of the Lord, and Jesus
findings.description: The speaker describes how Jesus consolidated multiple Hellenistic mediator traditions (Son of God, Son of Man, Messiah) into a unified figure, with the divine name as central to how these titles functioned to assert divine authority and power.
findings.quote: the early tradition of Jesus—which consolidates some of these figures, Son of God, Son of Man, Messiah, prophet, priest, king, all these things—consolidates all of these different traditions within the figure of Christ, and the divine name is central to the New Testament's presentation of Jesus identity with God as well.

findings.video_title: Does the Bible say the Savior will be God?
findings.description: The speaker notes that the concept of a divine "mediatory figure" who manifests divine power only became conceivable in Greco-Roman Judaism, directly supporting the book's thesis that understanding Jesus requires a Greco-Christian rather than purely Jewish framework.
findings.quote: it wouldn't have been possible until Greco Roman period Judaism when they're starting to contemplate the idea of mediatory figures who in some sense presence the deity and bear the dei's power and authority

findings.video_title: Didaskaloi’s next online class: The Gospel of Mark
findings.description: The transcript cites evidence that Mark 1:1 ("the beginning of the good news of Jesus Christ") directly parallels imperial language from the Priene inscription (~9 BCE) referring to Augustus's birthday as "the beginning of the good news for the world." This demonstrates how early Christian authors used Greek imperial titulature and political-theological language to position Jesus as ruler, supporting the argument that "Son of God" and kingship claims should be read through Hellenistic royal-institutional frameworks.
findings.quote: the gospel of Mark might start off throwing shade at the Roman Emperor it begins the beginning of the good news of Jesus Christ which sounds an awful lot like an inscription from a place called Prine in turkey that was done around 9 BCE that refers to the birthday of the god Augustus as the beginning of the good news for the world that came by reason of him so the author of Mark might be trying to suggest that Jesus is the actual ruler of this world

findings.video_title: Come say hi!
findings.description: McClellan cites Paul Holiday's commentary to argue that Philippians 2's hymn reflects the Hellenistic conceptual template of the "name-bearing angel," showing Jesus's exaltation and divine status are expressed through Greco-Jewish frameworks.
findings.quote: particularly the hymn in Philippians 2 seems to reflect that idea of Jesus um not as an angel but taking that conceptual template of the name bearing Angel and saying Jesus is doing the same thing

findings.video_title: Livestream!
findings.description: Gospel terminology from imperial politics: The speaker notes that Mark likely borrowed the genre of "gospel" (euangelion) from the praetio inscription used for Augustus, which referred to bringing "Justice and Order and to deliver his people." This supports the book's argument that Greek political language shaped how Jesus was presented.
findings.quote: I think Mark who's probably one of The Originators of the way we understand it certainly in the in the sense of the genre of is probably I think borrowing it from the pr inscription which uses it to refer to uh the god Augustus coming into the world his birthday as the good news that uh he was there to uh to bring Justice and Order and to deliver uh his people

findings.video_title: Livestream!
findings.description: Binitarianism as framework for divine titles: The speaker describes "binitarianism" in Greco-Roman period Judaism—where exalted figures or principal Angels function as "God's uh second in command their their agent"—as the context for understanding Jesus's divine identity.
findings.quote: you have something that some people described as binitarianism where you have uh exalted humans or you have principal Angels uh who kind of occupy uh you know they're on deck when it comes to the Divine their God's uh second in command their their agent you have that going on within GRE period Judaism that I think is what gives rise to the notion of Jesus as God

findings.video_title: Livestream!
findings.description: Divine agency pattern: Jesus fits into a tradition of Greco-Roman Jewish divine agents (angels, Metatron) who possess God's name and function as a "communicable vehicle of divine agency," a pattern extending to New Testament understandings of Jesus.
findings.quote: this concept of the name indwelling the angel is kind of like the idea of a uh communicable vehicle of divine agency um and and I think that's probably what's going on with uh Jesus in the New Testament particularly with these references to Jesus possessing the Divine name being given the Divine name

findings.video_title: What’s the Book of Enoch?
findings.description: The Book of Enoch, written within Hellenistic Judaism, profoundly influenced early Christian theology, with the NT concept of "son of man" drawing directly from Enochic sources. This demonstrates how Hellenistic Jewish theological frameworks informed NT messianic titles and theology.
findings.quote: the notion of the son of man is pulling from the enochic tradition

findings.video_title: Doesn’t John 1:1 tell us the Word was God?
findings.description: The video demonstrates that the Gospel of John employs logos theology—a Greek philosophical framework originating with Philo of Alexandria—to articulate the divine identity and relationship of Jesus/the Word. This supports the thesis that early Christian theology, including theological titles and their meaning, was fundamentally articulated through Hellenistic philosophical frameworks rather than exclusively Jewish categories.
findings.quote: And so John 1:1 is expressing logos theology which we find earlier in Pho of Alexandria. And then we find developing between Justin Martyr and Aanagoras and Theophilles of Antioch and Erynaeus and others.

findings.video_title: On the Significance of the Name Barabbas
findings.description: Matthew 27:16 uniquely names the prisoner as "Jesus Barabbas" (meaning "Jesus, son of the father"), creating a deliberate contrast with "Jesus who they call the Messiah." The gospel author's conscious construction of this titular contrast evidences substantive use of "son" and messianic language as meaningful distinctions.
findings.quote: Matthew says his name is Jesus [Barabbas]" and "Matthew seems to be adding the name Jesus to bring that conceptual contrast closer to the surface" between "Jesus son of the father" and "Jesus the Messiah.

findings.video_title: Does this “debunk” the Trinity?
findings.description: The transcript traces how the Logos doctrine—a Hellenistic philosophical framework for understanding divine mediation—became the foundational lens through which early Christians explained Jesus's divine identity. This directly supports the book's claim that Greco-Christian frameworks were essential to understanding Jesus from the beginning.
findings.quote: the doctrine of the logos originated it is a borrowing from a kind of stoic middle platonic idea of God's mediation with the material world

findings.video_title: Does this “debunk” the Trinity?
findings.description: The transcript documents how Justin Martyr and early apologists explicitly adopted Greek philosophical categories (Logos Endiatetos vs. Logos Prooros) to articulate Jesus's identity, and how Justin referred to Jesus as "another God" while maintaining monotheism—a distinctly Hellenistic solution.
findings.quote: Justin Martyr explicitly says that they are distinct in number but not in will and calls the logos multiple times another God

findings.video_title: Does this “debunk” the Trinity?
findings.description: The evidence that Greek philosophical rationalization took explicit priority over scriptural interpretation in developing Christian theology of Jesus validates the book's argument that a Greco-Christian framework, not a Judeo-Christian one, best explains early Christianity's self-understanding.
findings.quote: the doctrine of the Trinity developed as early Christians were integrating Greek philosophy and their Jewish Christian ideas about monotheism and the philosophical side of things took priority

findings.video_title: Does this “debunk” the Trinity?
findings.description: Philo's pre-Christian use of the "second God" terminology for the Logos establishes the Hellenistic royal/divine terminology context in which Jesus's identity was articulated.
findings.quote: Philo talks about the God who appears to Moses and to others being the logos and being quote a second God

findings.video_title: Did I misread John?
findings.description: Mlelen argues that logos theology—the Hellenistic philosophical framework articulated by Philo—is the essential interpretive lens for understanding John's Christology. This supports the book's thesis that Greco-Christian frameworks are necessary for understanding Jesus and early Christianity.
findings.quote: the clearest one is the logos framework which Pho talks about at some length and which most scholars agree is to one degree or another undergirling what the author of the gospel of John is trying to say.

findings.video_title: Livestream!
findings.description: Discussion of Philo's adoption of Platonic-Stoic cosmology to understand logos as divine mediation demonstrates how Greek philosophical frameworks were used to interpret divine authority. The speaker notes that John similarly elaborates this framework to understand Jesus's divine role and creative authority through logos.
findings.quote: Philo saw the logos as the mediator the bridge between the spiritual Divine and uh the fleshly temporal world... John is kind of uh elaborating on a very similar idea uh and I think the uh where the logos is the means the tool of uh creation and that every everything that God did in the world was done through the logos

findings.video_title: What does “Son of Man” refer to?
findings.description: The scholar confirms that "Son of God" is a title originating from the Greco-Roman world and was specifically used to designate Caesar and other human figures claiming divinity, directly supporting the book's argument that this title should be understood as a Hellenistic royal designation.
findings.quote: Son of God comes primarily from the Greco Roman world and it was used to refer to the Caesar and so it was primarily a title applied to humans who had some kind of pretensions to divinity.

findings.video_title: Some Thoughts on John 1:1c
findings.description: The proper Greek interpretation of John 1:1c—reading theos without the definite article as "divine" rather than "God"—exemplifies the book's methodological argument that early Christian texts must be read within their original Greco-Christian grammatical and semantic categories, not through later theological retroejection.
findings.quote: the end of john 1 1 does not say the word was god it says the word was divine... if the author wanted to say the word was god they would have included the definite article because in greek predications if you have a definite noun preceding the verb that noun takes the article

findings.video_title: Patreon Livestream
findings.description: McClellan discusses evidence that Greco-Roman period Judaism had already developed the theological framework of an intermediary figure (the "son of man") who is closely identified with God, manifests divine presence, and receives worship—precisely the kind of religious concept that contributed to early Christian Christology and supports the book's thesis about Hellenistic religious categories influencing Jesus theology.
findings.quote: this idea of an intermediary figure who is closely identified with God Bears God's name uh manifests God's presence and is able to receive worship uh I think certainly is part of GRE or Roman period Judaism

findings.video_title: What happened to my beard?!?
findings.description: McClellan explains that divine titles like "God" applied to rulers in the Hellenistic context did not require strict monotheism, but rather reflected the practice of deifying rulers as entities (not necessarily THE God). This supports understanding "Son of God" as a Hellenistic royal title rather than a strictly monotheistic claim.
findings.quote: God does not necessarily mean God the father or the god of Israel but just a way to refer to an entity as Divine as a deity

findings.video_title: What happened to my beard?!?
findings.description: McClellan is organizing a conference on why "monotheism is not a productive analytical framework for looking at the Bible," directly supporting the book's foundational premise that early Christianity should be understood through non-monotheistic, Greco-Christian frameworks.
findings.quote: the idea is why is monotheism not a productive analytical framework for looking at the Bible

findings.video_title: What happened to my beard?!?
findings.description: McClellan notes that first-century Greek-Roman Judaism interpreted Old Testament prophecies through the Septuagint (Greek translation), demonstrating how Greek linguistic and cultural frameworks shaped early Christian theological interpretation rather than Hebrew-based Jewish frameworks.
findings.quote: they interpreted the prophecies much differently... particularly because they were um interpreting everything through the light of the septu the ancient Greek translation of the Hebrew Bible

findings.video_title: Maklelan Livestream
findings.description: McClellan discusses the Prienne inscription (dated ~100 BCE) referencing "the good news of the coming into the world of the god Augustus," noting that Mark's opening—"the good news of Jesus Christ, Son of God"—likely echoes this Roman imperial language. This supports the thesis that "Son of God" carries Hellenistic/Roman imperial theological weight.
findings.quote: this might be riffing on an inscription that dates to around a hundred years before called the Prienne inscription which talks about the good news of the coming into the world of the god Augustus. Um and it uses that same word uh uh that now we know as Avangelon or Evangelios.

findings.video_title: Maklelan Livestream
findings.description: McClellan recommends Michael Pepper's *The Son of God in the Roman World*, which he describes as analyzing "Mark and Christologology and the idea of uh adoption in the Roman world and how gods function in the Roman world and how the idea of a son of God uh function in the Roman world." This directly supports Chapter 3's argument about "Son of God" as a Hellenistic royal title operating within Greco-Roman political theology.
findings.quote: Michael Pepper's book, The Son of God in the Roman World, talks an awful lot about Mark and Christologology and the idea of uh adoption in the Roman world and how gods function in the Roman world

findings.video_title: Maklelan Livestream
findings.description: McClellan cites John and Adela Collins' *King and Messiah as Son of God*, describing it as discussing "how the context of the development within Judaism of of their messianism and uh the idea of a son of god and how hellenistic and greor roman cultures influence that." This supports the Greco-Christian framework for understanding Jesus's titles.
findings.quote: John and Adela yarro Collins which talks a little bit about how the context of the development within Judaism of of their messianism and uh the idea of a son of god and how hellenistic and greor roman cultures influence that

findings.video_title: Maklelan Livestream
findings.description: McClellan recommends Michael Bird's *Jesus Among the Gods: Early Christologology in the Greco Roman World*, noting it "covers a variety of different uh texts and a variety of different approaches to understanding Jesus's relationship to God" and makes "a case that there is um that there was a concern for the ontological relationship of Jesus to God already by the time of the composition of the gospel of John." This reinforces both the Greco-Roman context (Chapter 3) and Johannine theological development (Chapter 4).
findings.quote: Michael Bird's book, Jesus Among the Gods: Early Christologology in the Greco Roman World... it's making um a case that there is um that there was a concern for the ontological relationship of Jesus to God already by the time of the composition of the gospel of John

findings.video_title: Is Paul the real founder of Christianity?
findings.description: The scholar discusses Paul drawing on already-circulating Greco-Roman period Jewish theological concepts to formulate Christology—specifically the logos from Philo, divine mediators, and image christology as foundations for understanding Jesus's divine nature. This supports the book's argument that early Christian theology was built on Greek intellectual frameworks, not solely Jewish ones.
findings.quote: Paul was taking up and perhaps incrementally elaborating on already circulating GRE Roman period Jewish ideas about Divine mediation whether it's through the logos which is already pre-existing in Philo...and image christology...Divine Images as mediators of God's agency and power

findings.video_title: There’s no monotheism in the Bible
findings.description: Mcleland demonstrates that fundamental Christian theological concepts like monotheism were Greek philosophical innovations retroactively imposed on biblical texts by post-biblical thinkers, supporting the thesis that Christian theology was fundamentally shaped by Hellenistic frameworks rather than purely Jewish traditions.
findings.quote: the Bible acknowledges the existence of numerous other gods from beginning to end... it is Thoroughly postbiblical and builds upon first Greek philosophical ideas about what deity is and specifically the notion that it is singular and unified and superlative and these are primarily post-biblical Innovations

findings.video_title: Is this the real Lord’s Prayer they didn’t tell us about?
findings.description: The speaker emphasizes that all Aramaic versions of the Lord's Prayer are translations from Greek, supporting the book's argument for the primacy of Greek sources in understanding Jesus's teachings within a Greco-Christian framework.
findings.quote: all Aramaic versions of the Lord's Prayer are translations from the Greek

findings.video_title: Thoughts on Psalm 82 and Its Use in John 10
findings.description: maklelan demonstrates that the Greco-Roman period fundamentally reshaped biblical interpretation and theological categories. John 10 employs Logos theology—a Greek philosophical framework—to articulate Jesus's claim to divine sonship, exemplifying the Greco-Christian synthesis necessary for understanding how Jesus's titles and identity were constructed.
findings.quote: Logos theology is at the root of the gospel of John... I am the Word of God. I have been sanctified. I have been sent into the world... how can you say that I am blaspheming by saying 'I am the Son of God'

findings.video_title: Responding to the claim the Trinity is "unavoidably biblical"
findings.description: The speaker extensively discusses "image christology," citing Alexi Kazazadon's scholarly work on divine images in Mediterranean cult contexts. This framework understands Jesus as a divine image that made "accessible the power, The Authority, the very presence of the deity"—a fundamentally Hellenistic religious model that supports interpreting Jesus's divine titles within a Greco-Mediterranean institutional context rather than purely Jewish categories.
findings.quote: Jesus is a Divine image of God because what do Divine Images do they make accessible the power The Authority the very presence of the deity that they index and they can be referred to both as the deity and not the deity this is the most parsimonious the most likely the best explanation for why it was intuitive and natural for a lot of people to think of Jesus as both not God and also in some sense presencing God

findings.video_title: Responding to the claim the Trinity is "unavoidably biblical"
findings.description: Scholarly analysis of Philippians 2 (Boers, Perryman, Holloway, Fredrickson) interprets "form of God" as Jesus filling a functional role or divine appearance rather than sharing essence. This functional understanding of divine titles aligns with Hellenistic royal and mediatorial frameworks.
findings.quote: Scholars are in pretty widespread agreement that this does not mean that Jesus shared one Essence with God but just that Jesus had the outward appearance or filled the role of a deity the way that God would

findings.video_title: Psalm 82 and why Jesus quoted it
findings.description: The Gospel of John's presentation of "Son of God" as a defensible claim relates to the book's argument about this title's theological and political significance. Mlelen shows that the Gospel interprets Psalm 82 through a Greco-Roman lens to argue that receiving the word of God can render humans divine, providing scriptural precedent for Jesus's claim to divine sonship.
findings.quote: The author of John 10 is having Jesus interpret Psalm 82 according to a later Greco Roman and rabbitic Jewish interpretation... participation in divinity can render someone divine... Jesus is using this passage to indicate there is nothing inappropriate about him identifying as the son of God.

findings.video_title: Maklelan Livestream!
findings.description: McClellan discusses Jesus being syncretized with other divine figures in the Greco-Roman world and argues that Mark's "good news" language parallels Augustus inscriptions, with Mark employing adoption christology closely related to Greco-Roman divine mediation frameworks.
findings.quote: there would have been plenty of people within early Christianity who...were probably like yeah is um Jesus is like Zeus or whatever...the gospel of Mark begins that begins with the good news uh of Jesus Christ um and this seems to be this kind of resonates with the prein uh inscription which comes from a little bit before uh Jesus's birth which which boasts about or talks about the good news of the coming of uh the god Augustus...a good discussion of how that um indicates that Mark has kind of an adoption christology that's closely related to the GRE Roman world

findings.video_title: Maklelan Livestream!
findings.description: McClellan is developing work on "image christology"—the idea that Greco-Roman logic of Divine Images governed how Jesus was conceptualized as manifesting God's presence. He cites recent scholarship (Alexi Konu) on how Greco-Roman Divine Image logic shaped Paul's christology, which he argues parallels his own work.
findings.quote: I'm starting up a paper that I'm going to be presenting at a conference in Italy in June on um Jesus uh on uh basically image christology the idea that the logic of Divine Images is what governed the conceptualization of Jesus as manifesting God's presence...Alexi konu recently published a paper talking about how the broader Greco Roman logic of uh Divine Images in forms on Paul's christology and I've argued something very similar

findings.video_title: Who are the gods of Psalm 82?
findings.description: McKlelan argues that interpretations of divine titles (particularly "gods" in Psalm 82 and, by extension, "Son of God" in John 10) fundamentally shifted during the Greco-Roman period. The author of John uses a Hellenistic-influenced reinterpretation of Psalm 82 to defend Jesus's divine status, showing how Greco-Roman context reshaped early Christian theology about Jesus's identity.
findings.quote: we've gone through the Greco Roman period and the understanding of deity has changed...the interpretation of what gods were changed...the author of John 10 is doing...using that interpretation of Psalm 82 to defend Jesus's identification as a Divine being that is not how the psalm was originally written to be understood

findings.video_title: Does Titus 2:13 Say Jesus is God?
findings.description: The speaker analyzes Titus 2:13's Hellenistic imperial language, showing that "God and Savior" and "great God and Savior" terminology was applied to Roman Emperors (Ptolemy, Augustus Caesar, Julius Caesar), and that the concept of "epiphanies" referred to the Emperor as a manifestation of divine presence. The author of Titus adapts this imperial theological framework to Jesus, strengthening the book's argument about Hellenistic royal titulature and Greek institutional context shaping early Christology. This addresses the book's THIN area of "comparative titulature across empires."
findings.quote: this language is Thoroughly Hellenistic this language is frequently used to refer to Emperors as the manifestation of the presence of God...we have Ptolemy we have Augustus Caesar we have Julius Caesar all referred to as God and savior great God and savior and even our great God and savior...the author of Titus is pulling from Hellenistic conceptualizations of uh significant individuals as manifesting the presence of God

findings.video_title: Is the title Lucifer related to the word halal?
findings.description: Jerome's rendering of Jesus as the morning star using the Greek term "phosphoros" (the Venus deity from the Greco-Roman pantheon) demonstrates how early Christian translators applied Greek/Greco-Roman religious terminology to Jesus, supporting the thesis that Greco-Christian categories were central to understanding him.
findings.quote: Lucifer is not a name it is a title... it is a reference to the deity that represents Venus in the Greco Roman Pantheon and that was Jerome's choice for rendering the Greek phosphoros... Jesus is twice represented as the morning star in the new testament... and that is rendered in the Vulgate by Jerome as Lucifer

findings.video_title: Did Jesus fulfill Isaiah 7:14 if he wasn’t named Emmanuel?
findings.description: The speaker demonstrates that Matthew had to adapt Hebrew prophecy (Isaiah 7:14) through Greek translation and terminology (specifically *parthenos* for "young woman" and reworking the grammatical subject from singular "she" to plural "they") to make it relevant to a Greco-Roman Jewish audience. This supports the book's argument about the necessity of Greco-Christian frameworks for understanding early Christian theology. The speaker further explains that fulfillment operates through Greek concepts of divine manifestation and authority rather than literal naming.
findings.quote: in order for this to still be at all relevant to anyone in Greco-Roman period Judaism we've got to tweak it a little bit

findings.video_title: Jesus’ Relationship with God is Borrowed
findings.description: The speaker catalogs evidence from Greco-Roman Jewish sources (Exodus, Daniel, Enochic literature, Talmud) showing that intermediary figures—the angel of the Lord, the son of man, Yahoel, and Metatron—possessed divine names, performed divine functions, and received worship. This supports the book's argument about comparative divine titulature in the Greco-Roman world and how Jesus's status claims drew on existing Greco-Roman Jewish theological templates rather than being unique.
findings.quote: we have other figures who do similar things... they have God's name and there's even a talmudic text... this idea that you could have an authorized Bearer or possessor of the Divine name who can deploy the name who can identify with the name who can do what only God is supposed to be able to do who can receive worship that already existed within Greco-Roman period Judaism and all those Traditions are consolidated within the figure of Jesus

findings.video_title: Am I wrong about Mark & Matthew’s christology?
findings.description: maklelan cites Michael Pepper's "The Son of God in the Roman World: Divine sunship in its social and political context" as authoritative scholarship on Mark's christology, emphasizing that understanding "Son of God" requires attention to its Greco-Roman political and social context—precisely the Greco-Christian framework the book advocates.
findings.quote: if you really want to understand what's going on here I would recommend you look at Michael Pepper's book The Son of God in the Roman World Divine sunship in its social and political context which is all about Mark and christology

findings.video_title: Am I wrong about Mark & Matthew’s christology?
findings.description: maklelan argues that Mark deliberately exploits the ambiguity of the Greek term "Kurios" (Lord) to suggest Jesus bears God's authority and manifests God's presence while remaining subordinate to God—not identifying him as Adonai himself. This interpretation aligns with the book's emphasis on understanding "Lord" and "Son of God" as Hellenistic royal titles rather than claims of divine nature.
findings.quote: the Greek the word is just Kurios which just means Lord which can be used as a substitution for the tetragramaton but can also be used to refer to other individuals who can bear the title Lord but are not Adonai the god of Israel... the author is not identifying Jesus as Adonai the god of Israel but they are exploiting the ambiguity of this generic term Kurios for their own rhetorical purposes which are about suggesting that Jesus in some way shape or form Bears God's Authority and even manifest God's presence but is inferior and subordinate to God

findings.video_title: Am I wrong about Mark & Matthew’s christology?
findings.description: maklelan notes widespread scholarly consensus (Joel Marcus, Eugene Boring, Adela Yarbro Collins, Daniel Johansson, Michael Pepper) that Mark and Matthew do not identify Jesus as God but rather deliberately maintain ambiguity about his relationship to divine authority, supporting the book's position that Greco-Roman institutional and political contexts are essential for proper interpretation.
findings.quote: if you look in the critical scholarship you will see they all are in widespread agreement that the author is not identifying Jesus as Adonai the god of Israel

findings.video_title: Did “they” take 2 sentences out of John 16 in the 4th century?
findings.description: The transcript emphasizes that the New Testament texts were "originally composed in Greek," supporting the book's methodological prioritization of Greek sources and frameworks over Judeo-Christian (Aramaic-based) interpretive approaches.
findings.quote: every last Aramaic word in the text has been translated from a Greek original because all of the New Testament was originally composed in Greek

findings.video_title: The fundamental contradiction of the Trinity
findings.description: The video documents how Greek philosophical frameworks were employed by apologists from the earliest post-apostolic period to articulate theological problems around Jesus's divine status. The problem of "Jesus being worshiped and not being God" requiring philosophical reconciliation through Greek metaphysical categories demonstrates the foundational importance of Hellenistic concepts to explicating Jesus's significance and theological status.
findings.quote: Because this started with the apologists trying to account for what was going on with Jesus being worshiped and not being God, but also in some sense being God... apologists were trying to make the gospel palatable to the Greco Roman intelligencia.

findings.video_title: December Livestream
findings.description: McClellan discusses Divine images in Greco-Roman antiquity as a conceptual framework for understanding Jesus's divine status. He argues this explains how early Christians could hold the paradoxical view of Jesus as both God and distinct from God, directly supporting the book's argument about understanding Jesus through Greco-Christian rather than purely Jewish theological categories.
findings.quote: Jesus manifests the father not because he is the father even trinitarians reject that but because he is the authorized possessor of the Divine name and just like a Divine image he bears and transmits the father's Power Authority and even his very presence... this is the intuitive logic of Divine Images that just makes the idea of Jesus as both God and not God feel natural to folks

findings.video_title: “I & the Father are one” doesn’t mean Jesus is God
findings.description: McClellan argues that "I and the Father are one" (John 10:30) does not mean Jesus is claiming to be God in the later Christian theological sense. By emphasizing that John 17 shows this same "oneness" is communicable to all followers, McClellan supports the book's project of reframing Christological titles away from later Christian theological (Trinitarian) interpretations toward alternative frameworks—consistent with the book's Greco-Christian institutional approach.
findings.quote: we can look in John 17 where Jesus prays three times in the intercessory prayer that his followers become one with him just like he is one with the father in other words whatever this Oneness is that Jesus shares with God is communicable and Jesus is explicitly praying for his own followers to achieve Oneness with him that is identical to the Oneness that he has with the Father

findings.video_title: What does “Son of Man” mean?
findings.description: McClellan confirms that "Son of God" draws on Greco-Roman imperial concepts, where the Emperor is designated "Son of God," corroborating the book's thesis that this title should be understood through a Hellenistic-royal framework.
findings.quote: Son of God is kind of riffing on the Greco Roman notion of the Emperor as a Son of God

findings.video_title: On Jesus’ relationship to the Angel of YHWH
findings.description: The scholar argues that Jesus was conceptualized as the "authorized bearer of the Divine name" through Greco-Roman Jewish Divine Images logic, granting him authority to exercise divine power—a theological framework that predates and differs from post-biblical Christian philosophical rationalization.
findings.quote: Jesus is conceptualized using the template of the logic of Divine Images and rationalized as the authorized bearer of the Divine name and thus the one who has the authority to act in God's name to exercise God's power and even to manifest God's presence

findings.video_title: Come say hi!
findings.description: McClellan discusses Jesus functioning as a "Divine image"—a material medium manifesting God's presence and power—which relates to understanding early Christology in Greco-Roman theological frameworks.
findings.quote: I think probably worshiped in the same way that a Divine image was worshiped that this is the material entity that uh manifest God's presence and power and so we can access God and worship God through this material media

findings.video_title: Mark 2 is Not Saying Jesus is God
findings.description: The speaker demonstrates that "Son of Man" authority claims should be understood within the Greco-Roman period Jewish tradition of authorized bearers of divine name and power (Yahoel, Metatron), supporting the book's argument that Jesus's titles function as institutional roles rather than deity claims, and reinforcing the necessity of a Greco-Roman Jewish framework for interpreting his authority.
findings.quote: this tradition is further developed in Greco-Roman period Judaism with figures like yahuwahil and Metatron and the son of man all of whom exercise God's power and authority because they are the authorized bearers of the Divine name... Jesus is incrementally elaborating on traditions in Greco-Roman period Jewish literature and consolidating all of these different streams of tradition within a single figure

findings.video_title: February Livestream!
findings.description: Mark 1:1's "gospel" language directly parallels imperial "gospel" announcements about Augustus, demonstrating how early Christians adapted Greco-Roman imperial-theological rhetoric to position Jesus as the worthier recipient of worship within this political-theological framework.
findings.quote: Mark starts off Mark 1:1 the beginning of the good news of Jesus Christ and this seems to be riffing on the Prine inscription which comes from many years before that but it's talking about Augustus... there's a lot of ways that Jesus is being held up as kind of the the more true uh Emperor worthy of worship

findings.video_title: February Livestream!
findings.description: Early NT Christologies engaged directly with Greco-Roman Jewish philosophical concepts. Paul drew from "Greco Roman period Judaism" featuring pre-existent Divine beings possessing the divine name, adapting the "Greek metamorphosis narrative" to describe Christ's incarnation and exaltation. John incorporated Philo's Stoic logos theology, which Philo explicitly terms a "second God."
findings.quote: Paul is probably adopting from GRE Roman period Judaism this kind of amalgamation of uh of different figures who are pre-existing who are uh lead Angels who are in possession of the Divine name... Paul is drawing from the uh Greek metamorphosis narrative to talk about how this pre-existent entity is incarnated in Jesus... John... kind of taking up and adapting uh pho's kind of stoic emanation idea about the logos which Pho refers to as a second God

findings.video_title: Come say hi!
findings.description: McClellan discusses Mark 1:1-3 as appropriating imperial political language from the Priene inscription praising Augustus. Mark frames Jesus using the same "good news" (evangelios) language used for the emperor's coming into the world, directly supporting the argument that "Son of God" and related titles should be understood as Hellenistic royal terminology rather than purely theological language.
findings.quote: this verse is actually riffing off of an inscription that is praising Augustus called the Prine inscription where it Praises it talks about the good news the evangelios uh of the coming into the world of the god Augustus uh and so mark one is kind of ripping that off and kind of appropriating for Jesus this kind of political praise that is aimed at uh the emperor

findings.video_title: Come say hi!
findings.description: Maklelan explains that the Logos tradition in Philo's Greco-Roman Judaism provided the theological framework early Christians drew upon to understand Jesus as the divine intermediary. This adoption of Hellenistic theological categories over purely Jewish ones demonstrates how Greco-Roman conceptual categories shaped Christological titles.
findings.quote: I think it was probably because there was already a tradition that identified uh the main like God's main um intermediary between the world of the Divine and the world of the flesh as the logos that's we what we find in Philo... at least with Philo uh and more Greco Roman oriented Judaism the logos was was the main one... I think that was just the the shortest way to connect those dots within early Christianity

findings.video_title: Feuding Gospel Authors: Apocryphal Epistles Clash over Imitations of Homer, Euripides, and Vergil
findings.description: McDonald demonstrates that Mark deliberately constructs Jesus as an "alternative founding myth" comparable to Virgil's Aeneas, motivated explicitly by anti-Roman political sentiment. This supports the book's argument that Gospel theological claims about Jesus and Kingdom should be understood as Greco-Roman political-theological constructs rather than purely Judeo-Christian ones.
findings.quote: Virgil's Aniid is understood to be the dominant founding myth of the Roman Empire, the early empire. And Mark is competing with that...it's informed by a hostility for Rome and it's expressed in an alternative founding myth.

findings.video_title: History or Not in 1 Corinthians 15
findings.description: Richard Miller's comparative analysis of apotheosis narratives in the Greco-Roman world provides concrete precedents for understanding Jesus's resurrection within Hellenistic royal deification frameworks. The discussion notes that Caesars and Romulus underwent apotheosis with three-to-four-day intervals and eyewitness testimony—structural parallels to early Christian resurrection claims that support understanding the resurrection as a political-theological legitimacy claim rather than purely Jewish apocalyptic expectation.
findings.quote: when I look at other narratives about Romulus having eyewitness testimony... I also know that there are narratives with Caesars having apotheosis to three days four days after they die... if you're gonna have a deification apotheosis narrative as we see in First Corinthians 15 having eyewitnesses is an important facet to that story

findings.video_title: How The Ancient Greek Stories Impact The Jesus Story
findings.description: The speaker provides historical evidence that "Son of God" (divi filius) was used as a Hellenistic/Roman royal title, exemplified by Augustus as an adopted son of Julius Caesar. This directly supports the book's argument that "Son of God" should be understood as a Hellenistic royal title rather than exclusively Jewish messianic language.
findings.quote: so some people have suggested and i think it's not bad that augustus the most venerated of the early emperors was an adopted man and that julius caesar was considered a god and so he was devi filius he was the son of god

chapter_inventory.argument: Jesus must have been more than a Judean royal claimant, because royal pretenders attract only their own nation, not foreigners.
evidence: Judas the Galilean led a purely Jewish resistance with no Greek following (Josephus, Ant. 18.1-10; War 2.117-118)
Second Temple Judaism shows no manuscripts, converts, or traces of Jesus or his followers

chapter_inventory.argument: The Christian movement spoke Greek and framed Jesus in the political-religious idiom Greeks already used for rulers.
evidence: Galilee as cross-cultural borderland on Via Maris, looking toward Tyre/Sidon and Decapolis
Sepphoris and Tiberias as Greek-facing administrative hubs
Jesus's travel toward Tyre and Sidon; encounter with Syrophoenician woman
Gospel of Matthew tradition of sojourn in Egypt
Acts 17:6-7: politarchs of Thessalonica accuse missionaries of proclaiming 'another king, Jesus'

chapter_inventory.argument: The titles given to Jesus (Son of God, King of Kings, Lord of Lords, Savior, etc.) were pre-existing political terms of Greek and Roman kingship, not novel theological inventions.
evidence: List of titles: Son of God, King of Kings, Lord of Lords, Savior of the World, Light of the World, Prince of Peace, Lamb of God, Good Shepherd, Way/Truth/Life, Alpha/Omega
Alexander claimed descent from Zeus-Ammon; coins with horns of Ammon (Plutarch, Lives 27)
Ptolemies styled as 'saviors' and 'manifest gods'
Seleucids adopted Epiphanēs ('God Manifest')
Augustus as divi filius on coins and civic festivals
Cities hailed emperors as sōtēr for grain, protection, peace

chapter_inventory.argument: The Hasmonean dynasty adopted Hellenistic royal theology, claiming divine favor and using Greek-style coinage and symbols.
evidence: John Hyrcanus remembered as beloved of God with prophetic gifts (Josephus, Ant. 13.299-300)
Alexander Jannaeus coins inscribed 'Jonathan the King' in Seleucid style (Josephus, Ant. 13.320)
Hyrcanus I coins (135 BCE) with five-pointed star and Seleucid anchor
Josephus describes them with titles like 'savior' and 'chosen of God' (Josephus, Ant.)

chapter_inventory.argument: Dead Sea Scroll 4Q246 applies 'Son of God' and 'Son of the Most High' to an eschatological ruler before any Christian usage.
evidence: 4Q246 text with 'Son of God' and 'Son of the Most High' titles

chapter_inventory.argument: Early Christian apologists explicitly argued that Jesus's divine sonship used the same categories as Greek divine sonship.
evidence: Justin Martyr, First Apology 21-22: Christians 'propound nothing different from what you believe regarding those whom you esteem sons of Zeus' (Heracles, Asclepius, Dionysus, Perseus)
Theophilus of Antioch cites Heracles ascending after the pyre and Asclepius struck by thunderbolt
Via Latina catacomb: Heracles raising Alcestis beside Jesus raising Lazarus

chapter_inventory.argument: Psalm 2:7 functions as a royal investiture formula applied to Jesus at three events (annunciation, baptism, resurrection), not as a statement about eternal essence.
evidence: Luke 1:32-35 (annunciation)
Western text of Luke 3:22 (baptism): 'You are my Son. Today I have begotten you'
Acts 13:33 (resurrection)

chapter_inventory.argument: Jewish archives and repositories are completely silent about Jesus and early Christianity, despite extensive overlap in themes.
evidence: Qumran: 900+ manuscripts from 250 BC to 70 AD (Tov catalogue)
Temple precincts archives of laws, genealogies, decrees
Alexandrian Judaism: Philo and extensive Greek exegesis pre-50 AD
Philo describes Therapeutae copying/storing sacred books (De Vita Contemplativa)
Schools of Hillel and Shammai generated halakhic material
No Gospel or Christian treatise in any Jewish repository
Shared themes: monotheism, 'voice in the wilderness', apocalyptic, messianic hopes, Philo's Logos doctrine
Josephus mentions James brother of Jesus (Ant. 20.200), executed 62 AD
Ebionites and Nazarenes only appear in 2nd-century notices as marginal groups

chapter_inventory.argument: The earliest extant Christian record is entirely Greek: Paul's letters, Didache, 1 Clement, Ignatius, and the Gospels. No securely dated Hebrew or Aramaic Christian texts exist.
evidence: Paul's letters from 50s AD to Greek-speaking assemblies (Corinth, Thessalonica, Galatia, Philippi, Rome)
Didache, 1 Clement (~96 AD), Ignatius all in Greek
Every extant Christian manuscript for the first two centuries is Greek

chapter_inventory.argument: The identification of God the Father with YHWH was not fixed in the first Christian generations; Paul and John describe the Father in Greek philosophical categories.
evidence: Paul at Athens quotes hymn to Zeus: 'For we are indeed his offspring' (Acts 17:28, Aratus Phaenomena 5)
Paul's Areopagus speech: God does not live in temples, not like gold/silver (Acts 17:24-28) — language of Plato and Stoics
Gospel of John presents Jesus as Logos, term from Greek philosophy
Philo of Alexandria developed Logos theology before NT (De Opificio Mundi)
Clement of Alexandria (Stromata 5.12): 'God is one and the same, the universal Father'
Fifth-century Christian epitaph invoking both 'ruler of Olympus' and Christ
chapter_inventory.section: Was God the Father the God of Moses or the God of Plato? (lines 97-126)

chapter_inventory.argument: Gnostics and Marcionites both explicitly denied that the Father was YHWH, proving the earliest proclamation was received in Greek categories.
evidence: By 2nd century: three branches — Gnostics, Marcionites, proto-Roman church
Gnostics: God of Moses is lower/ignorant power
Marcionites: God of Moses is evil
Roman church remained ambiguous, speaking of Father in both Jewish and Greek terms
chapter_inventory.section: Was God the Father the God of Moses or the God of Plato? (lines 119-126)

chapter_inventory.argument: Jesus consistently teaches the Greek (Platonic) model of the immortal soul, not the Hebrew nefesh that perishes with the body.
evidence: Hebrew nefesh: breath perishing with body, descending to Sheol
Greek psyche: immortal essence surviving body
'Do not fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul'
'What does it profit a man to gain the world and forfeit his soul?'
'Today you will be with me in Paradise' — immediate post-mortem consciousness
Parable of Lazarus and rich man: two men aware, speaking after death
Transfiguration: Moses and Elijah alive and conscious centuries after death
chapter_inventory.section: Anthropology: Jesus's doctrine of the soul (lines 127-144)

chapter_inventory.argument: The Lord's Prayer (Pater Noster) avoids Jewish covenant markers and fits Egyptian solar-royal liturgy clause by clause.
evidence: Two Gospel forms: Matthew 6:9-13 and Luke 11:2-4
Absence of Sinai, Torah, Zion, Abraham, Sabbath, sacrifices
Universal Father vs. covenant God who brought out of Egypt
Sanctified Name without Tetragrammaton
Kingdom descending from heaven (cosmic axis), not Davidic restoration on Zion
Daily bread: Great Hymn to Aten praises god who 'daily makes bread for humankind'
Forgive debts: Egyptian judgment — heart weighed against feather of Ma'at
Peirasmos as eschatological ordeal; Evil One as devourer (Ammit in Egyptian iconography)
Doxology basileia/dynamis/doxa on Greek inscriptions honoring Ptolemaic, Seleucid, and Roman rulers
'Amen' phonetic overlap with Amun (both written with consonants *MN*); Alexander claimed descent from Zeus-Ammon

chapter_inventory.argument: Matthew's 'Father who sees in secret' is the precise title and theology of Amun ('the Hidden One'), forming a smoking gun connection.
evidence: Matthew 6:4, 6:6, 6:18 refrain: 'your Father who sees in secret will reward you'
Amun = 'the Hidden One' (title and theology)
Lord's Prayer framed within almsgiving/prayer/fasting trio with 'hidden Father' description
